worldview.holiness: God is holy (set apart). His people and spaces must be holy too. Uncleanness was the opposite of holiness.
worldview.life_and_death: Most uncleanness connected to death or loss of life (corpses, disease, blood loss). God was the God of life.
worldview.boundaries: Purity laws created visible boundaries between Israel and other nations, and between sacred and common.

customs.sources: Uncleanness came from: dead bodies, skin diseases ('leprosy'), bodily discharges (blood, semen), certain animals, childbirth.
customs.contagion: Uncleanness could spread. Touching something unclean made you unclean. This affected beds, chairs, dishes.
customs.cleansing: Wait a set time (often 7 days). Wash body and clothes. Sometimes offer sacrifice. Priest pronounces clean.
customs.isolation: Severely unclean people (lepers) lived outside the camp and had to warn others by calling 'Unclean!'

key_interactions.summary: Detailed laws about clean/unclean animals, diseases, discharges, childbirth
Syrian commander healed by washing in Jordan seven times
Jesus touches leper—shocking reversal; instead of becoming unclean, Jesus makes him clean
12 years of bleeding meant 12 years of isolation; Jesus heals and calls her 'daughter'
It's what comes out of a person that defiles, not what goes in
Sheet of unclean animals; 'What God has made clean, do not call unclean'—opens door to Gentiles

peoples.name: Firstborn Rights (Birthright)
peoples.description: The firstborn son held special status. He received a double portion of inheritance (twice what other sons got), led the family after the father died, and carried on the family name. This birthright could be lost or transferred.
peoples.biblical_role: Explains Jacob buying Esau's birthright, why Reuben lost his status, and the significance of Israel being called God's 'firstborn.'

worldview.primacy: The firstborn represented the family's strength and was the first fruit of the father's vigor (Gen 49:3).
worldview.responsibility: With privilege came responsibility. The firstborn cared for parents in old age and led the extended family.

customs.double_portion: Firstborn inherited twice the share of other sons. If three sons, estate divided in four: firstborn got two parts, others one each.
customs.redemption: Israel's firstborn belonged to God after the Exodus. They had to be 'redeemed' (bought back) with payment.
customs.transfer: Birthright could be lost through sin (Reuben) or sold (Esau). Father could sometimes choose a younger son (Solomon over Adonijah).

key_interactions.summary: Esau trades birthright for stew—'despised his birthright'
Isaac's blessing meant for firstborn goes to Jacob through deception
Reuben lay with father's concubine; birthright given to Joseph's sons (1Ch 5:1)
Tenth plague kills all Egyptian firstborn; Passover protects Israel's
All Israel's firstborn belong to God; must be redeemed with payment
God calls Israel 'my firstborn son'—special status among nations

peoples.name: Gleaning Rights
peoples.description: Landowners were required to leave the edges of fields unharvested and not pick up dropped grain. This 'leftover' belonged to the poor, widows, orphans, and foreigners. It was law, not charity—the poor had a right to it.
peoples.biblical_role: The setting for Ruth meeting Boaz. Gleaning shows God's provision for the vulnerable built into Israel's social structure.

worldview.gods_ownership: The land ultimately belonged to God. Israelites were tenants. God required they share with those who had no land.
worldview.dignity: Gleaning let poor people work for food rather than beg. They maintained dignity while receiving provision.

customs.field_edges: Don't harvest to the very edge of your field. Leave the corners for the poor.
customs.dropped_grain: Don't go back for dropped or forgotten sheaves. Leave them.
customs.vineyards_orchards: Same rules for grapes and olives. Don't strip every branch or pick up fallen fruit.

key_interactions.summary: Leave corners of field and fallen grapes for poor and foreigners
Forgotten sheaf belongs to widow, orphan, foreigner—'so God may bless you'
Ruth gleans in Boaz's field; he shows extra kindness; leads to marriage
Disciples pick grain on Sabbath—likely from field edges designated for travelers

peoples.name: City of Refuge
peoples.description: Six cities where someone who killed accidentally could flee for safety. The 'blood avenger' (victim's family) had the right to kill a murderer. But accidental killers could escape to these cities and live safely until the high priest died.
peoples.biblical_role: Shows how Israel balanced justice with mercy. The killer was protected but not free—a middle ground between execution and no consequence.

worldview.blood_justice: Blood required blood. Murder polluted the land. But intent mattered—accident was different from murder.
worldview.clan_responsibility: Families were responsible to avenge their dead. This prevented endless feuds by providing a legal framework.

customs.six_cities: Three east of Jordan, three west. Spaced so anyone could reach one within a day's journey.
customs.trial: The killer had to stand trial. If it was murder (intentional), handed over to the blood avenger. If accidental, stayed in the city.
customs.confinement: Accidental killers lived in the city until the high priest died. Then they could go home safely.
customs.roads: Roads to these cities were well-maintained with clear signs. Nothing should slow down someone fleeing for their life.

key_interactions.summary: God commands six cities; explains difference between murder and accident
Moses designates Bezer, Ramoth, and Golan east of Jordan
Joshua designates Kedesh, Shechem, and Hebron west of Jordan
Killer remains until high priest's death—then can go home with no vengeance

peoples.name: Sandal Ceremony
peoples.description: Removing and giving a sandal was a legal act that transferred rights or finalized agreements. It served as a physical witness to a transaction in a culture where few could read or write.
peoples.biblical_role: Appears in Ruth when the closer relative refuses to redeem, and in the law about refusing levirate marriage. Also connected to ideas of possession and authority.

worldview.symbolic_transfer: Walking on land with your sandal showed ownership. Giving your sandal transferred that right to another.
worldview.public_witness: These ceremonies happened at the city gate before witnesses. Everyone knew who had rights to what.

customs.redemption_transfer: When refusing the right to redeem property, a man removed his sandal and gave it to the other party.
customs.levirate_refusal: If a man refused to marry his brother's widow, she removed his sandal and spit in his face—public shaming.
customs.servant_status: Carrying or untying another's sandals was slave work. John the Baptist said he wasn't worthy to do this for Jesus.

key_interactions.summary: Closer relative removes sandal, giving Boaz the right to redeem Ruth
Widow removes sandal, spits; his house called 'house of the unsandaled'
'Remove your sandals—you're on holy ground.' Bare feet showed humility before God.
'I am not worthy to carry his sandals'—less than a slave compared to Jesus

peoples.name: Foot Washing
peoples.description: In a world of dusty roads and sandals, feet got dirty. Washing a guest's feet was basic hospitality—but it was slave's work. No free person washed another's feet. Jesus doing this for his disciples shocked them.
peoples.biblical_role: Explains why Peter resisted when Jesus washed his feet, and why the sinful woman washing Jesus' feet with tears was so significant.

worldview.hospitality: Providing water for foot washing was minimum hospitality. Not offering it was an insult.
worldview.status: This was the lowest household task. Slaves did it, not free family members.

customs.arrival: Guests washed their feet upon entering a home—either themselves or attended by a slave.
customs.reclining: People reclined on couches to eat, feet extended. Clean feet mattered for the dining room.

key_interactions.summary: Abraham offers water to wash the visitors' feet—proper hospitality
Jesus notes Simon didn't offer water for his feet—the sinful woman made up for it
Jesus takes slave's role; Peter objects; Jesus says 'you also should wash one another's feet'
Godly widow known for 'washing the saints' feet'—humble service

peoples.name: Anointing with Oil
peoples.description: Pouring olive oil on someone's head set them apart for a special role. Kings, priests, and prophets were anointed. 'Messiah' (Hebrew) and 'Christ' (Greek) both mean 'Anointed One.'
peoples.biblical_role: Explains the title 'Christ,' the significance of Mary anointing Jesus, why kings were anointed, and anointing the sick with oil.

worldview.setting_apart: Anointing marked someone as chosen by God for a special task. The Spirit of God came upon the anointed.
worldview.honor: Oil was a sign of abundance and blessing. Anointing someone's head was honoring them.

customs.kings: Prophets anointed kings, signifying God's choice. Samuel anointed Saul, then David. Oil poured from a horn.
customs.priests: Aaron and his sons anointed for priestly service. Oil mixed with blood sprinkled on them.
customs.hospitality: Anointing a guest's head was special honor. Not doing it could be seen as a slight.
customs.healing: Anointing the sick with oil accompanied prayer for healing (Jam 5:14).

key_interactions.summary: Samuel pours oil on Saul's head; Spirit rushes on him; he prophesies
Samuel anoints David among his brothers; Spirit of the LORD upon him from that day
Woman pours expensive perfume on Jesus' head at Simon's house
Mary anoints Jesus' feet with nard, wipes with hair—house filled with fragrance
'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and power'
'Anoint them with oil in the name of the Lord' for healing

peoples.name: Tax Collectors (Publicans)
peoples.description: Jews who collected taxes for Rome. They bid for contracts, then collected more than required and kept the difference. Grouped with 'sinners' because they worked for the occupiers, handled Gentile money, and often cheated people.
peoples.biblical_role: Jesus ate with tax collectors, shocking the religious leaders. Matthew was a tax collector before becoming a disciple. Zacchaeus was a chief tax collector who repented.

worldview.roman_system: Rome auctioned tax collection rights. Collectors paid Rome upfront, then collected from the people—whatever extra they got was profit.
worldview.social_status: Considered traitors for working with Rome. Their money was considered unclean. Their testimony wasn't accepted in court.
worldview.religious_view: Lumped with sinners and prostitutes as the worst of society. Pharisees wouldn't eat with them.

social_structure.hierarchy: Chief tax collectors supervised regions and employed collectors under them. They were wealthy but despised.
social_structure.toll_collectors: Stationed at borders, ports, and roads. Collected customs duties on goods passing through.

customs.collection_points: Tax booths set up on major roads and at city gates. Matthew sat at his booth when Jesus called him.
customs.extortion: No fixed rates meant collectors could demand whatever they wanted. John the Baptist told them to collect only what was required (Luk 3:13).

key_interactions.summary: Jesus calls Matthew from his tax booth: 'Follow me.' Matthew gets up and follows.
Jesus dines at Matthew's house with tax collectors; Pharisees ask why he eats with sinners
Chief tax collector climbs tree to see Jesus. Jesus stays at his house. Zacchaeus repays those he cheated.
Parable: Tax collector beats his chest saying 'God have mercy on me, a sinner'—goes home justified, not the Pharisee
Tax collectors ask John what to do. 'Collect no more than what is appointed.'

peoples.name: Shepherds
peoples.description: Those who tended sheep and goats. Once an honorable occupation (Abraham, Jacob, Moses, David were shepherds), by New Testament times they were looked down on. They couldn't keep Sabbath properly, their work made them ceremonially unclean, and they couldn't testify in court.
peoples.biblical_role: God is called Shepherd of Israel. David went from shepherd to king. Jesus is the Good Shepherd. Angels announced Jesus' birth to shepherds first—not to the religious leaders in Jerusalem.

worldview.ancient_honor: The patriarchs were shepherds. Moses tended sheep for 40 years before God called him. David was taken from the sheep to be king.
worldview.later_stigma: By Jesus' time, rabbis listed shepherds among those who couldn't give testimony. They were suspected of grazing on others' land.
worldview.god_as_shepherd: The LORD is my shepherd' (Psa 23). Israel is the flock. Leaders who failed were called 'worthless shepherds.'

social_structure.hired_vs_owner: Owner shepherds cared for their own flock. Hired shepherds worked for wages and might flee from danger.
social_structure.responsibilities: Leading to pasture and water, protecting from predators, finding lost sheep, carrying lambs, treating wounds.

customs.night_watch: Sheep folded at night in pens or caves. Shepherds took turns watching. Good shepherds slept at the gate.
customs.rod_and_staff: Rod for defense against predators, staff with crook for guiding and rescuing sheep from ravines.
customs.counting: Each sheep passed under the rod for counting. Shepherds knew each by name.

key_interactions.summary: David tells Saul he killed a lion and bear while protecting sheep—training for Goliath
Angels announce to shepherds keeping night watch. They go find the baby and spread the news.
Jesus: 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.'
Shepherd leaves 99 to find 1 lost sheep. 'More joy in heaven over one sinner who repents.'
Son of Man separates nations 'as a shepherd separates sheep from goats'

peoples.name: Lepers (Those with Skin Diseases)
peoples.description: People with various skin diseases (the Hebrew word covered more than just leprosy). Declared 'unclean' by priests, they lived outside camps and towns, had to warn others by calling 'Unclean!', and couldn't enter the Temple. Healing required priestly inspection and sacrifice.
peoples.biblical_role: Leprosy pictured sin's effect—it excluded people from God's presence and community. Jesus touched lepers and healed them, showing his power over uncleanness and his compassion for outcasts.

worldview.uncleanness: Not about hygiene but about being cut off from God's presence. Unclean people couldn't approach the tabernacle or temple.
worldview.contagion: Anyone who touched a leper became unclean. Lepers lived apart to prevent spreading uncleanness.
worldview.shame: Being a leper meant social death. Family and community contact severed. Some saw it as divine punishment.

social_structure.exile: Lepers lived outside city walls, often in colonies. They depended on charity.
social_structure.identification: Torn clothes, loose hair, covered upper lip. Required to call out 'Unclean!' when people approached.

customs.priestly_inspection: Only priests could declare someone leprous or clean. Detailed inspection process in Leviticus 13-14.
customs.cleansing_ritual: After healing, elaborate ritual: two birds (one killed, one released), seven days waiting, shaving, washing, sacrifices.
customs.isolation_period: Even after healing, stayed outside camp 7 days, washed clothes, then examined again.

key_interactions.summary: Miriam speaks against Moses; struck with leprosy; shut out of camp 7 days then healed
Syrian commander healed by dipping in Jordan seven times. 'Now I know there is no God except in Israel.'
Starving lepers find Aramean camp abandoned—God had routed the army
Leper: 'If you will, you can make me clean.' Jesus touches him. Immediately clean.
Jesus heals ten lepers. Only one returns to thank him—a Samaritan.
Jesus dines at house of Simon the leper in Bethany (presumably healed)

peoples.name: Fishermen
peoples.description: Those who caught fish for food and trade. On the Sea of Galilee, fishing was a major industry. Fishermen worked at night (fish came to surface), used nets or lines, and sold their catch fresh or salted. Several of Jesus' disciples were Galilean fishermen.
peoples.biblical_role: Jesus called fishermen as his first disciples, saying 'I will make you fishers of men.' Peter, Andrew, James, and John left their nets to follow him. Jesus performed miracles involving fish and fishing.

worldview.honest_trade: Unlike tax collectors, fishing was respectable work. Hard labor but not dishonorable.
worldview.partnership: Fishing required cooperation—boats, nets, and crews. Peter and Andrew worked with James and John.
worldview.economic_importance: Dried and salted fish was a major Galilean export. Fishing rights could be leased.

social_structure.boat_owners: Families owned boats. Zebedee had hired servants, suggesting some wealth.
social_structure.hired_workers: Not all fishermen owned boats—some worked as hired hands.
social_structure.fish_merchants: Fish processed and sold through merchants. Magadan/Tarichaea means 'place of salted fish.'

customs.night_fishing: Fish came to surface at night. Nets cast in darkness, catch hauled in at dawn.
customs.net_types: Cast nets (circular, thrown by hand), drag nets (large, between boats), and trammel nets.
customs.sorting_catch: Good fish kept, bad thrown away—Jesus used this image for final judgment (Mat 13:47-50).

key_interactions.summary: Jesus sees brothers casting net: 'Follow me and I will make you fishers of men.' They leave nets immediately.
Jesus calls them from boat where they mend nets with father Zebedee. They leave boat and father.
After fishing all night with nothing, Jesus says cast on right side—nets break from fish. Peter: 'Depart from me, a sinful man.'
Boy's five loaves and two fish feed 5,000. Twelve baskets left over.
Disciples fish all night, catch nothing. Jesus on shore tells them where to cast—153 large fish.
Jesus tells Peter to catch a fish—it will have a coin for the temple tax

peoples.name: Widows and Orphans
peoples.description: Women whose husbands had died and children without parents. In a society where men owned property and earned wages, widows and orphans were extremely vulnerable. God commanded special care for them and judged nations that mistreated them.
peoples.biblical_role: God identifies himself as 'defender of widows' and 'father of orphans.' Care for widows and orphans is repeatedly commanded in the Law. The early church organized widow support. James says true religion includes caring for orphans and widows.

worldview.vulnerability: Without a husband or father, women and children had no legal protector, no income source, no inheritance rights.
worldview.divine_protection: God took up their cause. Mistreating them brought his judgment. Blessing them brought his favor.
worldview.community_duty: Israel was commanded to leave grain in fields for widows to glean, include them in festivals, provide for them from tithes.

social_structure.kinsman_redeemer: Nearest male relative could marry a widow (levirate marriage) to provide for her and raise up heirs.
social_structure.family_responsibility: Sons should support widowed mothers. Paul says believers must care for widowed relatives.
social_structure.community_care: Gleaning rights, tithe sharing, and inclusion in community meals.

customs.mourning_dress: Widows wore distinctive clothing. Putting it aside signaled moving on (like Tamar in Gen 38:14).
customs.remarriage: Widows could remarry. Younger widows were encouraged to. Older widows served the church.
customs.gleaning: Widows had right to follow harvesters and pick up what was left. Ruth gleaned in Boaz's fields.

key_interactions.summary: Naomi loses husband and sons, left with daughter-in-law Ruth. Returns to Bethlehem destitute.
Starving widow feeds Elijah her last meal. Flour and oil don't run out until rain comes.
Prophet's widow about to lose sons to slavery for debt. Elisha multiplies oil to pay creditors.
Poor widow puts two small coins in treasury. Jesus: 'She gave more than all the others—everything she had.'
Jesus sees widow's only son being carried out dead. Raises him and gives him back to his mother.
Dispute over widow care leads to appointment of seven deacons to oversee daily distribution.
'Pure religion is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress'

peoples.name: Slaves and Servants
peoples.description: People bound to serve others. Hebrew slaves could be debt-bondsmen (released after 7 years) or foreign captives (permanent). Slavery in Israel was regulated by law—masters couldn't kill slaves, and escaped slaves weren't to be returned. Roman slavery was harsher with no time limits.
peoples.biblical_role: Many biblical figures were slaves or servants: Joseph in Egypt, Israelites in Egypt, Daniel in Babylon. Jesus 'took the form of a servant.' Paul called himself a slave of Christ. The NT doesn't abolish slavery but regulates the relationship.

worldview.hebrew_bondservant: Israelites who sold themselves due to debt served 6 years, freed in the 7th. Could choose permanent service.
worldview.foreign_slaves: Captured in war or purchased. Could become permanent property but still had protections under law.
worldview.roman_slavery: Slaves had no legal personhood. Could be freed (manumission) and become citizens. Many early Christians were slaves.

social_structure.household_servants: Trusted slaves ran households (like Joseph for Potiphar). Some held significant responsibility.
social_structure.debt_slaves: Israelites who couldn't pay debts could sell themselves. Released in Year of Jubilee at latest.
social_structure.freedmen: Former slaves could gain wealth and status. Some became patrons of churches.

customs.ear_piercing: Hebrew slave who chose to stay with master permanently had ear pierced at doorpost (Exo 21:6).
customs.jubilee_release: Every 50 years, Hebrew slaves freed, debts cancelled, land returned to original families.
customs.sanctuary: Escaped slaves not to be returned to masters—could live freely in Israel (Deu 23:15-16).

key_interactions.summary: Brothers sell Joseph to Midianite traders for 20 pieces of silver. Ends up in Egypt.
Egyptians make Israelites' lives bitter with hard labor—bricks, mortar, field work.
'If your brother becomes poor and sells himself, don't treat him as a slave. In Jubilee he goes free.'
Paul sends escaped slave back to Philemon—'no longer as a slave but as a beloved brother.'
Jesus 'emptied himself, taking the form of a servant (doulos)'
Paul instructs slaves and masters. Masters told to stop threatening—same Master in heaven.

peoples.name: Sinners and Prostitutes
peoples.description: 'Sinners' was a category for those who didn't follow the Law—either through occupation (tax collectors, shepherds) or behavior (prostitutes, thieves). Pharisees avoided contact with them to stay ritually pure. Jesus deliberately associated with them.
peoples.biblical_role: Jesus was criticized for eating with 'sinners' and told parables about God seeking them. He said tax collectors and prostitutes would enter the kingdom before the religious leaders. Rahab the prostitute is in Jesus' genealogy.

worldview.religious_outcasts: The religious establishment defined 'sinners' as those outside proper Jewish observance. Contact made you unclean.
worldview.economic_desperation: Many prostitutes were widows or abandoned women with no other means of survival.
worldview.public_shame: Sinners were publicly known. The woman in Luke 7 was 'a sinner in the city'—everyone knew who she was.

social_structure.excluded: Sinners couldn't testify in court, couldn't participate in synagogue, weren't invited to meals with the respectable.
social_structure.tax_collector_category: Lumped together: 'tax collectors and sinners' appears as a phrase—the worst of society.
social_structure.repentance_path: Sinners could repent and be restored. John the Baptist attracted many who came confessing sins.

customs.meal_fellowship: Eating with someone meant acceptance. Jesus eating with sinners scandalized Pharisees.
customs.ritual_purity: Righteous Jews avoided sinners to prevent contamination. Jesus reversed this—his purity spread to them.
customs.public_confession: Those seeking restoration made public confession and changed their ways.

key_interactions.summary: Rahab the prostitute hides Israelite spies, helps them escape. Saved when Jericho falls.
Known sinner washes Jesus' feet with tears, dries with hair, pours perfume. Jesus: 'Her many sins are forgiven.'
'Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?' Jesus: 'I came not for the healthy but the sick.'
Jesus to chief priests: 'Tax collectors and prostitutes are entering the kingdom ahead of you.'
Pharisees grumble about Jesus welcoming sinners. He tells three parables about God seeking the lost.
Teachers bring woman caught in adultery. Jesus: 'Let him without sin cast the first stone.' All leave. 'Go and sin no more.'

peoples.name: Proselytes (Converts to Judaism)
peoples.description: Gentiles who fully converted to Judaism. Men were circumcised, both men and women immersed in a mikveh, and they offered sacrifice. After conversion, they were considered Jews—part of the covenant community with all obligations.
peoples.biblical_role: Some proselytes appear in Acts (like Nicolas of Antioch). The Ethiopian eunuch and Cornelius were on the path to conversion. Debate over whether Gentile Christians needed to become proselytes was settled at the Jerusalem Council.

worldview.full_commitment: Proselytes cut all ties with former gods and people. They joined Israel completely.
worldview.legal_status: Treated as native-born Israelites in many respects. Could marry Jews (with some restrictions).
worldview.religious_motivation: Attracted by monotheism, moral code, and community. Some found paganism empty.

social_structure.conversion_process: Study period, circumcision for men, mikveh immersion, sacrifice at Temple. Rabbis examined motives.
social_structure.new_identity: Given a Hebrew name. Father now 'Abraham'—became children of Abraham by adoption.
social_structure.community_acceptance: Some rabbis said converts not to be reminded of former life. Others treated them as second-class.

customs.mikveh: Ritual immersion symbolized death to old life, birth into Israel. Witnessed by rabbis.
customs.circumcision: Adult circumcision was the main barrier. Required for full covenant membership.
customs.sacrifice: Brought offering to Temple as sign of joining God's people (before 70 AD).

key_interactions.summary: Ruth to Naomi: 'Your people shall be my people, and your God my God.' Becomes ancestor of David.
Among those hearing the apostles in their own languages: 'Jews and proselytes.'
One of the seven deacons—'a proselyte of Antioch.' Gentile convert serving the church.
Jesus: 'You travel over sea and land to make a single proselyte, then make him twice as much a child of hell.'
Debate: must Gentile believers become proselytes? Decision: no circumcision required—just basic requirements.

peoples.name: God-Fearers
peoples.description: Gentiles attracted to Judaism who worshipped the God of Israel and followed some Jewish practices but didn't fully convert. They attended synagogue, kept some commandments, but weren't circumcised. A significant group in the New Testament.
peoples.biblical_role: Many early Gentile Christians were God-fearers—already familiar with Scripture and Jewish ethics. Cornelius was a God-fearer. Paul often found them in synagogues, receptive to his message about Jesus.

worldview.partial_adherence: Worshipped Israel's God, rejected idolatry, kept moral commands. Stopped short of full conversion.
worldview.synagogue_attachment: Welcomed in synagogues as 'those who fear God.' Sat in a designated area. Could contribute financially.
worldview.circumcision_barrier: Adult circumcision was painful and dangerous. Also meant complete social break from Gentile world.

social_structure.respected_position: Often wealthy patrons who supported synagogues. Centurion in Luke 7 built a synagogue.
social_structure.liminal_status: Neither fully Jewish nor fully Gentile. Couldn't participate in all Jewish rituals.
social_structure.ready_converts: When Christianity offered full inclusion without circumcision, many God-fearers responded.

customs.sabbath_observance: Many kept the Sabbath and attended synagogue services.
customs.dietary_laws: Some followed Jewish food laws. Others kept only basic rules.
customs.charitable_giving: Often generous supporters of Jewish communities—gave alms, supported the poor.

key_interactions.summary: Centurion built the synagogue. Sends for Jesus to heal servant. Jesus: 'I haven't found such faith in Israel.'
Devout God-fearer, gave alms, prayed constantly. Peter sent to him. Holy Spirit falls—first Gentile converts.
Reading Isaiah on his way from Jerusalem. Philip explains—eunuch believes and is baptized.
In synagogue, Paul addresses 'Men of Israel and you who fear God'—two distinct groups present.
'A worshiper of God' from Thyatira, selling purple cloth. The Lord opens her heart to Paul's message.
God-fearer whose house next to synagogue became Paul's base in Corinth when Jews rejected him.

peoples.name: Samaritans (as Social Outcasts)
peoples.description: Descendants of Israelites who intermarried with foreigners after Assyria conquered the northern kingdom. Jews considered them half-breeds and heretics. They worshipped at Mt. Gerizim, not Jerusalem. Jews traveling between Galilee and Judea often crossed the Jordan to avoid Samaritan territory.
peoples.biblical_role: Jesus deliberately interacted with Samaritans—the woman at the well, the grateful leper, the Good Samaritan parable. The gospel spread to Samaria in Acts 8. Samaritans represented the ultimate outsider that Jews would never help.

worldview.mutual_hatred: Jews said Samaritans had no share in the resurrection. Samaritans sometimes attacked Jewish pilgrims.
worldview.religious_difference: Samaritans accepted only the five books of Moses. Worshipped at Mt. Gerizim, which Jews had destroyed.
worldview.ethnic_contempt: 'Samaritan' was an insult. 'Are we not right that you are a Samaritan and have a demon?' (Joh 8:48)

social_structure.geographic_separation: Lived in the region of Samaria. Most Jews avoided the area entirely.
social_structure.no_dealings: Jews wouldn't share utensils, wouldn't accept Samaritan hospitality, wouldn't intermarry.
social_structure.parallel_religion: Had their own temple (destroyed 128 BC), their own priests, their own version of the Torah.

customs.gerizim_worship: Claimed Mt. Gerizim, not Jerusalem, was the true place of worship (Joh 4:20). Still sacrifice Passover there today.
customs.torah_only: Didn't accept the Prophets or Writings—only the Pentateuch. Had their own ancient text.
customs.passover_sacrifice: Unique practice: still sacrifice lambs on Mt. Gerizim at Passover.

key_interactions.summary: Jesus asks Samaritan woman for water—shocking. 'Jews have no dealings with Samaritans.' Many Samaritans believe.
Priest and Levite pass by wounded man. Samaritan helps him. Jesus: 'Which was neighbor?' 'The one who showed mercy.'
Ten lepers healed; only one returns to thank Jesus—and he was a Samaritan.
Samaritan village won't receive Jesus. James and John: 'Shall we call fire from heaven?' Jesus rebukes them.
Philip preaches in Samaria; many believe. Peter and John come—Samaritans receive the Holy Spirit.
Jews to Jesus: 'Are we not right that you are a Samaritan and have a demon?'
